enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, peer-to-peer messenger with end-to-end encrypted chat, audio, and video calls and no central server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jami is a free/libre, end-to-end encrypted communication platform distributed as a GNU project and developed by Savoir-faire Linux. Unlike most chat apps, Jami is built on a fully peer-to-peer architecture: messages, calls, and file transfers travel directly between devices rather than through a company-run server, and accounts can be created without providing any personal information.

The platform covers instant messaging, one-to-one and group audio/video calls, screen sharing, media streaming, and audio/video message recording, all protected by end-to-end encryption with perfect forward secrecy and X.509-based key management. Jami also supports linking multiple devices to a single account, an extension/plugin system, and SIP compatibility for interoperating with traditional telephony systems.
Jami is licensed under the GNU GPLv3 or later and received the Free Software Foundation's Social Benefit Award in 2023.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Jami suits privacy-conscious individuals and organizations that want encrypted messaging and calling without depending on a third-party server, as well as developers who want to build on top of the Jami daemon or its plugin API for custom communication tools.

features.title: Peer-to-peer architecture
features.description: Messages and calls travel directly between devices instead of routing through a company-run server.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: Communications are protected with end-to-end encryption and perfect forward secrecy, using X.509-based key management.

features.title: Multi-device accounts
features.description: A single Jami account can be linked across multiple devices without a central account server.

features.title: Audio, video, and group calls
features.description: Supports one-to-one and group voice/video calls, screen sharing, and media streaming.

features.title: Plugin platform and SIP support
features.description: Extend Jami with plugins, or use it as a SIP client to interoperate with existing telephony systems.

features.title: Cross-platform clients
features.description: Official clients are available for Linux, Windows, macOS, Android, Android TV, and iOS.

pros: Completely free with no ads, subscriptions, or feature limits
No mandatory central server to maintain since it runs peer-to-peer
Strong end-to-end encryption with perfect forward secrecy
GNU GPLv3 licensed and backed by the Free Software Foundation
Accounts can be created without handing over personal information
cons: Peer-to-peer connectivity can be harder to establish behind strict NATs or corporate firewalls
Smaller user base than mainstream messaging apps, so fewer contacts are already on it
Directory-style enterprise management requires self-managed infrastructure (e.g., your own account manager or TURN server)

faqs.question: Is Jami free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jami is completely free, open source under GPLv3, and has no ads or paid tiers.

faqs.question: Does Jami require a server?
faqs.answer: No central server is required for messaging or calls since Jami is peer-to-peer by design. Organizations can optionally self-host components like an account manager, name server, or TURN server for NAT traversal.

faqs.question: What platforms does Jami support?
faqs.answer: Official clients exist for Linux, Windows, macOS, Android, Android TV, and iOS.

faqs.question: Can I do group video calls in Jami?
faqs.answer: Yes, Jami supports group audio and video calls along with screen sharing.

faqs.question: Who maintains Jami?
faqs.answer: Jami is developed by Savoir-faire Linux as a GNU project and is licensed under GPLv3 or later.

use_cases.title: Privacy-first team communication
use_cases.description: Distributed teams that want encrypted messaging and calls without routing traffic through a third-party company server.

use_cases.title: Personal secure messaging
use_cases.description: Individuals who want end-to-end encrypted chat and calls with contacts, without creating an account tied to personal data.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jami is a free, open-source, peer-to-peer messenger with end-to-end encrypted chat, voice, and video calls across Linux, Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source live chat and helpdesk software you can self-host for free, with optional AI bots, voice, video, and screen sharing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Live Helper Chat is an open-source live support platform for real-time customer communication, covering chat, voice, video, and screen sharing. It runs on PHP and MySQL, can be installed on standard web hosting, and the project states that enterprise-level features are free to use in the self-hosted edition, including unlimited operators, chats, and departments.

The platform integrates with AI services including OpenAI's ChatGPT, Rasa, Ollama, and Gemini for chatbot automation, and offers operator-to-operator chat, proactive visitor invitations, visitor tracking, screenshot capture, and file uploads. Optional enterprise-grade features (such as advanced search and scaling) can use Elasticsearch, Amazon S3, and Node.js.
In addition to the self-hosted, free-to-download software, Live Helper Chat offers a cloud-hosted option where the company runs the installation for customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Live Helper Chat fits support teams and businesses that want a full-featured live chat platform without per-agent SaaS fees, and are comfortable self-hosting a PHP/MySQL application, or that prefer letting the vendor host it for them.

features.title: Multi-channel support
features.description: Handle live chat, voice, video calls, and screen sharing from a single operator interface.

features.title: AI chatbot integrations
features.description: Connects to OpenAI ChatGPT, Rasa, Ollama, and Gemini for automated conversation handling.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps let operators respond to visitors on the go.

features.title: Unlimited operators and departments
features.description: The self-hosted edition supports unlimited operators, chats, and departments at no license cost.

features.title: Proactive engagement tools
features.description: Includes proactive chat invitations and operator-to-operator messaging.

features.title: Visitor tracking
features.description: Tracks visitor activity and supports screenshot capture and file uploads during conversations.

pros: Self-hosted edition is free and open source with enterprise-level features included
Full ownership of chat data and infrastructure
Built-in AI chatbot integrations across multiple providers
Native mobile apps for operators
Actively developed with WordPress-compatible installation
cons: Self-hosting requires managing a PHP/MySQL stack (and optionally Elasticsearch, S3, and Node.js for enterprise features)
Interface is less polished than modern SaaS live-chat competitors
Cloud-hosted pricing is not published upfront and requires contacting the vendor

faqs.question: Is Live Helper Chat free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted, open-source version is free to download and use, including features often reserved for paid tiers elsewhere.

faqs.question: Can I use Live Helper Chat without hosting it myself?
faqs.answer: Yes, Live Helper Chat also offers a cloud-hosted option where the vendor manages the installation for you.

faqs.question: What does Live Helper Chat require to self-host?
faqs.answer: A standard PHP and MySQL hosting environment; some enterprise features additionally use Elasticsearch, Amazon S3, or Node.js.

faqs.question: Does Live Helper Chat support AI chatbots?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with OpenAI ChatGPT, Rasa, Ollama, and Gemini.

faqs.question: Are there mobile apps for operators?
faqs.answer: Yes, Live Helper Chat provides iOS and Android apps.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted customer support
use_cases.description: Support teams that want a free, full-featured live chat platform with complete control over their data.

use_cases.title: AI-assisted chat support
use_cases.description: Teams that want to combine human operators with AI chatbots (ChatGPT, Ollama, Gemini, Rasa) for first-line responses.
use_cases.audience: Businesses adding automation to support workflows

features: Same core feature set as self-hosted edition

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Live Helper Chat is a free, open-source live chat and helpdesk platform you can self-host, with AI chatbot integrations, voice/video, and mobile apps.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, low-latency voice chat application with strong encryption, popular for gaming and community voice servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mumble is a free, open-source, low-latency, high-quality voice chat application. It uses the Opus codec together with a custom low-latency protocol capable of round-trip times as low as 10-30 milliseconds, and adds TLS encryption, positional audio for supported games, and a granular channel and ACL-based permission system for server administrators.

The server component, Murmur, is self-hosted: administrators run it on their own hardware or a VPS, with an official Docker image available for quick deployment. Both the client and server are free and open source under GPLv3, with no subscriptions, usage caps, or proprietary lock-in — the only cost is whatever hosting you choose to run it on.
Official clients are available for Windows, Linux, and macOS; the server can run on essentially anything that supports Qt.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Mumble is a strong fit for gaming communities that need low-latency, positional voice chat, and for privacy-conscious groups or organizations that want to self-host their own voice server instead of relying on a third-party platform.

features.title: Low-latency voice
features.description: Uses the Opus codec with a custom protocol to achieve round-trip latency as low as 10-30 milliseconds.

features.title: TLS encryption
features.description: Voice traffic is encrypted end-to-end between client and server using TLS.

features.title: Positional audio
features.description: Supports in-game positional audio for a number of games, so voice volume/direction matches in-game position.

features.title: Granular permissions
features.description: A detailed channel and ACL permission system lets server admins control access down to individual channels and actions.

features.title: Text chat and channel hierarchy
features.description: Includes built-in text chat alongside voice, organized into a channel hierarchy.

features.title: Official Docker image
features.description: The Murmur server can be deployed quickly using the official mumble-voip/mumble-docker image.

pros: Extremely low voice latency compared to most alternatives
Free and open source with no subscription or usage caps
Fine-grained channel and permission controls for server admins
Long track record of stability in gaming and community use
Full data control since you run your own server
cons: Client interface feels dated compared to modern chat apps like Discord
No official first-party mobile client (only community-built apps)
Text chat and community features are minimal compared to full chat platforms
Requires running and maintaining your own Murmur server

faqs.question: Is Mumble free?
faqs.answer: Yes, both the Mumble client and the Murmur server are free and open source under GPLv3.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Mumble?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Murmur server is designed to be self-hosted, including via an official Docker image.

faqs.question: What platforms does Mumble support?
faqs.answer: Official clients are available for Windows, Linux, and macOS.

faqs.question: Is there a Mumble mobile app?
faqs.answer: There is no official Mumble mobile client from the core project; mobile access relies on community-built apps.

faqs.question: How does Mumble compare to Discord for voice quality?
faqs.answer: Mumble is specifically engineered for very low latency voice, which is a key reason gaming communities have used it for over a decade, though it lacks Discord's broader social/chat feature set.

use_cases.title: Gaming community voice chat
use_cases.description: Communities that need reliable, low-latency, positional voice chat for multiplayer gaming.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team voice server
use_cases.description: Organizations wanting a private, encrypted voice server they fully control instead of a third-party SaaS platform.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mumble is a free, open-source, low-latency voice chat app with TLS encryption and positional audio, self-hosted via the Murmur server.

enrichment.short_description: Share sensitive information via self-destructing, one-time-view links, with an open-source self-hosted option and a free plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Onetime Secret is a service for securely sharing sensitive information through links that self-destruct after being viewed once or after a set expiration period. Secrets are protected with server-side encryption and can optionally require a passphrase for an extra layer of protection, with configurable expiration windows.

The codebase is open source and can be self-hosted, and the service also offers a hosted version with a free 'Basic' plan and a paid 'Identity Plus' plan that adds custom domains, branding, longer expiration windows, and team member access. Secrets can be created and retrieved either through the web interface or the REST API, which is available on all plans including the free one.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Onetime Secret suits IT, security, and support teams that need to send passwords, API keys, or other sensitive details without leaving them permanently in chat logs or email inboxes, whether they prefer to self-host or use the free hosted plan.

features.title: One-time viewable links
features.description: Secrets automatically self-destruct after being viewed once, or after a configurable expiration period.

features.title: Passphrase protection
features.description: Add an optional passphrase for an additional layer of security on a shared secret.

features.title: Server-side encryption
features.description: Secrets are encrypted server-side to protect data at rest.

features.title: Custom domains
features.description: The Identity Plus plan supports unlimited custom domains and custom branding for a white-labeled experience.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Create and retrieve secrets programmatically via the REST API, available even on the free Basic plan.

features.title: Open-source self-hosting
features.description: The full codebase is open source, letting teams run their own instance instead of using the hosted service.

pros: Focused, simple tool that solves a specific security problem well
Open source and self-hostable for teams that want full control
Free Basic plan includes REST API access
Reduces sensitive data lingering permanently in chat or email history
cons: Free plan is limited to one custom domain and a maximum 14-day expiration
Identity Plus paid plan is priced in EUR (€35/month) on the published pricing page
Not a full password manager — designed only for single-use, short-lived sharing

faqs.question: Is Onetime Secret free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a free 'Basic' plan (€0/month) with core secret-sharing features and REST API access; a paid 'Identity Plus' plan (€35/month) adds custom branding, unlimited domains, and longer expirations.

faqs.question: What happens after a secret is viewed?
faqs.answer: The secret is automatically destroyed after being viewed once, or after its configured expiration period passes, whichever comes first.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Onetime Secret?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project is open source and can be self-hosted instead of using the hosted service.

faqs.question: Is there an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, a REST API is available for creating and retrieving secrets programmatically, on both free and paid plans.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the free and paid plans?
faqs.answer: The free Basic plan supports one custom domain and expirations up to 14 days; the paid Identity Plus plan adds unlimited domains, custom branding, expirations up to 30 days, and unlimited team members.

use_cases.title: Sharing credentials securely
use_cases.description: Sending passwords, API keys, or tokens between team members without leaving them in permanent chat/email history.

use_cases.title: Support and onboarding handoffs
use_cases.description: Sending temporary access details to customers or new hires via a link that expires after one view.

features: Homepage access controls and Incoming Secrets

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Onetime Secret lets you share sensitive info via self-destructing, one-time-view links, with a free plan, REST API, and open-source self-hosting.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted one-time secret sharing tool with client-side AES-256 encryption so the server never sees your data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OTS (One-Time-Secret, developed as Luzifer/ots) is an open-source secret-sharing platform that encrypts secrets with symmetric 256-bit AES encryption directly in the browser before they ever reach the server. The server only ever stores the encrypted blob and a unique identifier, never the plaintext or the passphrase, and the secret is permanently deleted after it is read once.

The project supports pluggable storage backends, including an in-memory option for testing and Redis for persistent production use, and ships with a command-line client for creating and retrieving secrets from scripts. It is released under the Apache-2.0 license, and a free public instance is available to use without installing anything at ots.fyi.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OTS is aimed at developers and system administrators who want a lightweight, zero-knowledge, self-hostable alternative for sharing secrets, especially those who value CLI/scripting support over a feature-rich hosted product.

features.title: Client-side AES-256 encryption
features.description: Secrets are encrypted in the browser before being sent, so the server never has access to the plaintext.

features.title: One-time read then deletion
features.description: Each secret is permanently deleted immediately after it is retrieved once.

features.title: Pluggable storage backends
features.description: Supports an in-memory backend for testing and Redis for persistent production deployments.

features.title: Command-line client
features.description: Includes a CLI tool for creating and retrieving secrets, useful for scripting and automation.

features.title: Free public instance
features.description: A hosted instance at ots.fyi is available for anyone who doesn't want to self-host.

features.title: Apache-2.0 licensed
features.description: The project is fully open source, allowing self-hosting and modification.

pros: Zero-knowledge design — the server never sees your unencrypted secret
Lightweight and simple to self-host
Free public hosted instance available with no signup
CLI support makes it easy to integrate into scripts and pipelines
cons: Fewer polish features than larger competitors (no custom domains or team accounts)
Production deployments depend on Redis for persistent storage
Public-facing documentation on the site itself is minimal

faqs.question: Is OTS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OTS is free and open source under the Apache-2.0 license, and the public instance at ots.fyi is free to use.

faqs.question: How is my secret encrypted?
faqs.answer: Secrets are encrypted with symmetric 256-bit AES directly in the browser before being sent, so the server only ever stores encrypted data.

faqs.question: Can I self-host OTS?
faqs.answer: Yes, the source is available on GitHub (Luzifer/ots) and supports in-memory or Redis-backed storage.

faqs.question: Is there a public instance I can use without installing anything?
faqs.answer: Yes, ots.fyi is a free public hosted instance of the software.

faqs.question: Is there a CLI?
faqs.answer: Yes, OTS includes a command-line client for creating and retrieving secrets.

use_cases.title: Developer secret sharing
use_cases.description: Sharing API keys, tokens, or credentials with teammates via a disposable, zero-knowledge link.

use_cases.title: Lightweight self-hosted deployment
use_cases.description: System administrators who want a minimal, self-hosted alternative to larger secret-sharing platforms.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OTS (Luzifer/ots) is a free, open-source one-time secret sharing tool with client-side AES-256 encryption and a self-hostable server.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, encrypted peer-to-peer platform for private chat, mail, file sharing, forums, and channels between trusted friends.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RetroShare is a decentralized communication platform built around a Friend-to-Friend (F2F) network: users connect their own nodes directly to trusted contacts to exchange data without any central server or corporate infrastructure in between. Authentication uses PGP keys combined with TLS encryption and Perfect Forward Secrecy, and the network runs over TCP, UDP, UPnP/NAT-PMP, with optional DHT discovery.

On top of encrypted chat (including group chat rooms) and asynchronous mail that gets stored on friends' nodes while a recipient is offline, RetroShare provides decentralized, BitTorrent-style file sharing with anonymous tunnels that extend privacy beyond direct friends, plus distributed forums, subscribable channels for publishing files, and boards for sharing pictures and links with voting. Experimental VoIP and video calling are also included.

For added anonymity, RetroShare can optionally route traffic through Tor or I2P to hide a user's IP address even from their own friend nodes. It is free and open-source software, with no ads, hidden costs, or terms of service, and does not generate revenue for its developers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: RetroShare fits privacy-focused individuals and communities that want decentralized, encrypted communication and file sharing without depending on any central server or company, and who are comfortable with a more technical, trust-based network model.

features.title: Friend-to-friend encrypted network
features.description: Connects directly to trusted contacts' nodes using PGP authentication and TLS with Perfect Forward Secrecy — no central server involved.

features.title: Encrypted chat and mail
features.description: Includes private and group chat, plus asynchronous mail that is relayed and stored on friends' nodes while you're offline.

features.title: Decentralized file sharing
features.description: BitTorrent-like swarming file sharing with anonymous tunnels that protect privacy beyond your direct friends.

features.title: Forums, channels, and boards
features.description: Distributed discussion forums, subscribable file-sharing channels, and boards for sharing pictures and links with voting.

features.title: Optional Tor/I2P routing
features.description: Can route traffic through Tor or I2P to hide your IP address even from your own friend nodes.

features.title: Experimental VoIP/video calling
features.description: Supports experimental voice and video calls over the same encrypted network.

pros: Fully decentralized with no central server or company controlling the network
Strong privacy model via PGP authentication and optional Tor/I2P routing
No ads, fees, or terms of service
Combines chat, mail, file sharing, and forums in a single application
cons: Friend-to-friend model means reach is limited to people you're directly or indirectly connected to
VoIP/video calling is explicitly experimental
More technical setup than mainstream chat apps, with a smaller user base
No official iOS client

faqs.question: Is RetroShare free?
faqs.answer: Yes, RetroShare is completely free and open source, with no ads, fees, or hidden costs.

faqs.question: How does the friend-to-friend network work?
faqs.answer: You add trusted contacts as 'friends' using PGP key exchange, and your node connects directly to theirs; data can also relay through mutual friends depending on the feature.

faqs.question: Does RetroShare support Tor or I2P?
faqs.answer: Yes, RetroShare can optionally route connections through Tor or I2P to hide a user's IP address, even from their own friends.

faqs.question: What platforms does RetroShare support?
faqs.answer: RetroShare is available for Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android.

faqs.question: Can I use RetroShare for file sharing?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports decentralized, swarming file sharing along with anonymous tunnels for extended privacy.

use_cases.title: Decentralized community collaboration
use_cases.description: Privacy-focused communities exchanging files, forum posts, and messages without a central server.

use_cases.title: Encrypted personal communication
use_cases.description: Individuals wanting encrypted chat and mail resistant to centralized surveillance or shutdown.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: RetroShare is a free, open-source, encrypted peer-to-peer platform for private chat, mail, file sharing, forums, and channels between friends.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted chat server for building real-time messaging into mobile and web apps, with clustering built in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SAMA is an open-source chat server maintained by the SAMA-Communications organization, designed for developers who want to add real-time messaging to their own mobile or web applications instead of building chat infrastructure from scratch. The core server (sama-server) is built on uWebSockets.js, and the project also provides an official React client for the web and a Flutter client for mobile apps.

SAMA supports real-time messaging with rich media, group chats, multi-device sync, push notifications, and built-in clustering for scalability and high availability, alongside developer APIs and SDKs for integration. A public cloud sandbox at app.samacloud.io lets developers try the full stack before self-hosting it themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: SAMA is aimed at developers and product teams building chat features into their own mobile or web products who want a self-hosted, open-source alternative to hosted chat APIs like Stream or Sendbird.

features.title: Real-time messaging
features.description: Supports real-time chat with rich media attachments, powered by a uWebSockets.js-based server.

features.title: Group chats and user management
features.description: Built-in support for group conversations and user account management.

features.title: Multi-device sync
features.description: Keeps conversations in sync across multiple devices for the same user.

features.title: Push notifications
features.description: Delivers push notifications to keep mobile users informed of new messages.

features.title: Built-in clustering
features.description: Includes clustering support out of the box for scaling and high availability.

features.title: Official web and mobile clients
features.description: Ships with a React client for web apps and a Flutter client for mobile apps, maintained by the same organization.

pros: Fully open source and self-hostable
Ready-made React (web) and Flutter (mobile) clients reduce integration work
Clustering built in for scaling without extra tooling
Free to self-host with no per-seat licensing cost
Public cloud sandbox available for quick evaluation
cons: Primarily a developer toolkit rather than a ready-to-use end-user chat app
Requires engineering effort to deploy, secure, and customize for production
Smaller community and ecosystem than established commercial chat SDKs

faqs.question: Is SAMA free?
faqs.answer: Yes, SAMA is open source and free to self-host; a public cloud sandbox is also available for testing.

faqs.question: Can I try SAMA without installing it?
faqs.answer: Yes, the SAMA stack can be tested via the public cloud sandbox at app.samacloud.io.

faqs.question: What clients does SAMA provide?
faqs.answer: SAMA offers an official React web client and a Flutter mobile client, both maintained by SAMA-Communications.

faqs.question: Is SAMA suitable for production use?
faqs.answer: SAMA includes production-oriented features like clustering and push notifications, but as with any self-hosted infrastructure, deployment and security hardening are the responsibility of the operator.

faqs.question: Where is SAMA's source code?
faqs.answer: It is hosted on GitHub under the SAMA-Communications organization, including the sama-server, sama-client, and sama-client-flutter repositories.

use_cases.title: Embedded chat for apps
use_cases.description: Adding real-time chat to an existing mobile or web product without depending on a third-party hosted chat API.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted chat infrastructure
use_cases.description: Startups or companies that want to avoid per-seat or per-message pricing from hosted chat SDKs.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SAMA is an open-source, self-hosted chat server with React and Flutter clients for adding real-time messaging to mobile and web apps.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted screen sharing server for developers, built on WebRTC with a built-in TURN server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Screego (screego/server) is an open-source, self-hosted screen sharing tool built for developers who want good quality, low-latency screen sharing without a third-party SaaS tool. It's written in Go, compiles to a single dependency-free binary, and can also be deployed via Docker in under a minute.

The server supports multiple concurrent viewers through an SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit) architecture that routes video streams without re-encoding, preserving quality while keeping CPU overhead low. Media is transported over WebRTC, secured with DTLS for key negotiation and SRTP for encrypting the stream, and Screego includes its own built-in TURN server for NAT traversal. The project is released under the GPL-3.0 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Screego suits developer and engineering teams that want a quick, self-hosted way to share screens — for pair programming, remote debugging, or support — without relying on a third-party screen-sharing service.

features.title: Multi-user screen sharing
features.description: An SFU-based architecture lets multiple viewers watch the same shared screen without server-side re-encoding.

features.title: WebRTC with encryption
features.description: Screen sharing traffic is transported over WebRTC, secured with DTLS for key negotiation and SRTP for media encryption.

features.title: Built-in TURN server
features.description: Includes its own TURN server so connections can traverse NATs and firewalls without extra infrastructure.

features.title: Single-binary or Docker deployment
features.description: Deploys as a single Go binary with no dependencies, or via Docker in under a minute.

features.title: Low latency, high resolution
features.description: Designed for good quality, low-latency screen sharing suitable for technical work like code review.

features.title: Open source
features.description: Released under the GPL-3.0 license with an actively developed GitHub repository.

pros: Extremely simple to self-host as a single binary or Docker container
No dependency on a third-party screen-sharing service
Free and open source with no usage limits
Efficient SFU routing keeps server CPU load low even with multiple viewers
cons: No built-in persistent text chat or room history
Requires exposing your own server (and TURN port range) to the internet for remote use
Fewer collaboration features than commercial tools like Zoom or Microsoft Teams

faqs.question: Is Screego free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Screego is free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: How do I deploy Screego?
faqs.answer: You can run it as a single compiled Go binary or deploy it with Docker.

faqs.question: Does Screego need a separate TURN server?
faqs.answer: No, Screego includes its own built-in TURN server for NAT traversal.

faqs.question: Can multiple people view a shared screen at once?
faqs.answer: Yes, Screego uses an SFU architecture that supports multiple concurrent viewers efficiently.

faqs.question: What license is Screego released under?
faqs.answer: Screego is released under the GPL-3.0 open-source license.

use_cases.title: Developer screen sharing
use_cases.description: Teams that want quick, self-hosted screen sharing for pair programming or remote debugging.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious support tooling
use_cases.description: Organizations avoiding third-party screen-share SaaS tools for data-sensitive support sessions.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Screego is a free, open-source, self-hosted WebRTC screen sharing server for developers, deployable as a single binary or via Docker.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted XMPP transport/gateway that lets an XMPP server bridge conversations to other messaging networks such as IRC.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Spectrum 2 is an open-source instant messaging transport/gateway that runs alongside an XMPP server, letting users on that server chat with people on other IM networks. It's written in C++ on top of the Swiften XMPP library, is self-hosted, and is released under the GNU GPLv2 or later.

Historically Spectrum bridged to a wide range of proprietary networks such as ICQ, AIM, MSN, Yahoo, and Facebook chat, in addition to IRC and SIP-based SIMPLE. Many of those proprietary networks have since been shut down by their own operators over the years, so in practice today Spectrum 2's main remaining value is bridging XMPP to networks that are still running, such as IRC. The most recent tagged release (2.2.1) dates back to 2023, so it should be treated as a mature, slow-moving project rather than one under active feature development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Spectrum 2 is aimed at administrators who already run an XMPP server and want to federate it with other still-active chat networks, particularly IRC, rather than teams looking for a modern, actively developed chat platform.

features.title: XMPP transport architecture
features.description: Runs as a gateway alongside an existing XMPP server to bridge conversations to other networks.

features.title: IRC bridging
features.description: Connects XMPP users with IRC networks and channels.

features.title: C++/Swiften backend
features.description: Built on the Swiften XMPP library for performance and protocol compliance.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs entirely on infrastructure you control, alongside your own XMPP server.

features.title: Per-network backend modules
features.description: Individual backend modules handle the protocol translation for each supported network.

features.title: GPLv2+ open source license
features.description: Source code is publicly available and modifiable under the GPLv2 or later license.

pros: Free and open source
Lets XMPP administrators federate with other networks without switching platforms
Fully self-hosted, giving admins full control over the gateway
Modular backend design per network
cons: Most of the proprietary networks it was originally built to bridge (AIM, MSN, Yahoo, ICQ) have since shut down, narrowing today's practical use mostly to IRC
Development has slowed, with the latest tagged release dating to 2023
Requires XMPP server administration knowledge to deploy and configure
Niche use case compared to modern chat platforms

faqs.question: Is Spectrum 2 still usable today?
faqs.answer: Yes, it still runs, but many of the legacy proprietary IM networks it was built to bridge have since shut down, so its remaining practical use is mainly bridging XMPP to networks like IRC.

faqs.question: What networks can Spectrum 2 bridge to?
faqs.answer: It was designed to bridge XMPP to networks including IRC, and historically to now-defunct networks like ICQ, AIM, MSN, Yahoo, and Facebook chat.

faqs.question: Is Spectrum 2 free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the GPLv2 or later license.

faqs.question: Do I need my own XMPP server to use Spectrum 2?
faqs.answer: Yes, Spectrum 2 is a transport/gateway that runs alongside an existing XMPP server rather than as a standalone chat app.

faqs.question: Is Spectrum 2 actively maintained?
faqs.answer: The project's most recent tagged release was in 2023, indicating slower, maintenance-level development rather than active feature growth.

use_cases.title: XMPP-to-IRC bridging
use_cases.description: XMPP server administrators who want their users to be able to chat with people on IRC.

use_cases.title: Protocol consolidation
use_cases.description: Self-hosters wanting to consolidate chat across multiple protocols behind a single XMPP server.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Spectrum 2 is a free, open-source, self-hosted XMPP transport/gateway that bridges XMPP servers to other chat networks like IRC.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source Discord alternative for text, voice, and video chat, with a self-hosted deployment option.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Stoat is an open-source communication platform, built with a Rust backend, organized around community 'servers' with text channels, voice, and video, in the same style as Discord. The project was previously known as Revolt Chat and rebranded to Stoat in early 2025 following a trademark dispute over the 'Revolt' name; all official repositories moved to the 'stoatchat' organization on GitHub as part of the rebrand.

Every feature ships free: there are no subscription tiers, no server boosts, and no paywalled features gating functionality. Communities can either use the hosted service at stoat.chat, or self-host the entire platform on their own infrastructure using the official self-hosted deployment repository, so messages never have to leave servers a community controls. The project carried over its user base from Revolt, which had surpassed 600,000 registered users before the rebrand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Stoat is aimed at communities and teams looking for a free, privacy-respecting, open-source alternative to Discord or Slack, whether they want to use the hosted service or run it entirely on their own infrastructure.

features.title: Text, voice, and video channels
features.description: Community 'servers' organize conversations into text channels alongside voice and video chat.

features.title: Moderation tools
features.description: Includes moderation capabilities for managing community servers.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: An official self-hosted repository lets communities run Stoat entirely on their own infrastructure.

features.title: Hosted service option
features.description: Communities that don't want to manage infrastructure can use the hosted stoat.chat service instead.

features.title: Multi-platform clients
features.description: Clients are available for web, desktop, and mobile.

features.title: No paywalled features
features.description: All features are free with no subscription tiers or server boosts, unlike Discord Nitro.

pros: Completely free with no subscription tiers or paywalled features
Open source and self-hostable for full data control
Large existing community carried over from Revolt (600,000+ registered users pre-rebrand)
Actively developed with modern web, desktop, and mobile clients
cons: Rebrand from Revolt to Stoat (2025) can cause confusion with older links, guides, and integrations
Smaller ecosystem of bots and third-party integrations than Discord
Self-hosting requires managing and updating your own infrastructure

faqs.question: Is Stoat free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stoat is completely free with no subscription tiers, server boosts, or paywalled features.

faqs.question: What happened to Revolt Chat?
faqs.answer: Revolt Chat rebranded to Stoat in early 2025 after a trademark dispute over the 'Revolt' name; the project and its user base carried over to the new name.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Stoat?
faqs.answer: Yes, an official self-hosted deployment repository is available for running Stoat on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: What platforms does Stoat support?
faqs.answer: Stoat provides clients for web, desktop, and mobile.

faqs.question: Is Stoat open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stoat is open source, with its backend written in Rust.

use_cases.title: Discord alternative for communities
use_cases.description: Communities migrating away from Discord for privacy, cost, or philosophical reasons.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team chat
use_cases.description: Teams wanting a free, self-hosted, ad-free platform for text, voice, and video communication.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stoat (formerly Revolt Chat) is a free, open-source Discord alternative with text, voice, and video chat, offering a self-hosted deployment option.
